title: Candidate-set guided prompt group routing

queries: GraphRAG query focused graph retrieval evidence ordering
G-Retriever relevant subgraph retrieval graph prompt
Lost in the Middle language models long context order sensitivity

papers.title: G-Retriever: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Graph Question Answering
papers.claim: Graph evidence should be selected according to the query before generation rather than consumed as undifferentiated graph context.
papers.relevance: EXP180 and EXP183 show the active signal is in candidate-aware prompt evidence selection, but the salt-side task is sensitive to how that evidence is ordered.

papers.title: Graph Retrieval-Augmented Generation: A Survey
papers.claim: GraphRAG pipelines separate graph-guided retrieval from graph-enhanced generation and emphasize query-relevant evidence organization.
papers.relevance: A group-level routing pass is still graph-guided retrieval/organization before frozen-LLM consumption, without changing scorer form or prompt length.

papers.title: Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts
papers.claim: LLM use of evidence is sensitive to context position and ordering, so relevance ranking can help or hurt depending on how it disrupts useful structure.
papers.relevance: EXP183 suggests token-level reordering plus sample conditioning destroys salt-side structural order. Routing whole metadata groups while preserving intra-group order tests a less destructive evidence-ordering mechanism.

evidence.why_now: EXP180 candidate-set-only token routing failed fixed screening but improved item-incoterms to mrr=0.7432317302922772, above repeated scorer-collapse bands. EXP183 sample-conditioned routing improved user-churn but pushed item-incoterms down to mrr=0.6782075427827381, close to the known collapse band. The ablation consequence is to keep candidate-set-only guidance but reduce prompt order destruction by routing metadata groups/blocks instead of individual tokens.

hypothesis: For autocomplete multiclass tasks, candidate-set guidance is useful but fine-grained token reordering can destroy route-local structural evidence that item-incoterms needs. Sorting only metadata groups by candidate-set relevance while preserving original order inside each group should keep the useful EXP180 candidate-aware signal, avoid the EXP183 sample-conditioning collapse, keep prompt length fixed, leave the scorer unchanged, and remain Stage4-compatible through candidate-set proxy evidence-routing episodes.
notes: Draft created after EXP183/184/185 finalized failed.
Keep Stage 3 finetune-only; do not use --pretrain.
This is not a scorer near-variant: it changes only graph prompt evidence ordering before frozen-LLM consumption.
Do not scan routing top-k, prompt token budgets, residual scales, batching/chunk sizes, rank auxiliary weights, or route sampling.

Implementation plan: reuse the EXP180 candidate-set query from class-id/raw-label text embeddings, compute relevance for existing prompt tokens, aggregate relevance by token metadata group, sort groups by mean relevance, and preserve the original token order inside each group.
No prompt-length inflation and no O(L*C) LLM rollout; candidate text representations are computed once and group routing is tensorized over the existing prompt tokens.
Stage4 compatibility story: candidate-set group relevance can be learned later from source-database proxy categorical-value retrieval/ranking episodes without target-database-only fixed heads.

Implementation added on 2026-05-11 via prompt_candidate_group_routing: candidate-set relevance is aggregated by metadata group and groups are sorted while preserving original token order inside each group.
Pre-launch validation completed on 2026-05-11: py_compile, git diff --check, JSON validation, and remote item-incoterms shape/throughput sanity all passed.

Remote sanity check completed on 2026-05-11: a 64-step item-incoterms finetune-only run on lab25211 GPU6 completed cleanly with no --pretrain, reached roughly 6.0 train step/s with batch_size=4 (about 24 effective train items/sec), and eval/test ran around 12.15 it/s with val_size=1.

Launched on 2026-05-11 with packed batch-equivalent placement: EXP186 user-churn on GPUs 4,6 with per-rank batch 2 wave 0; EXP187 user-ltv on GPUs 4,6 with per-rank batch 1 wave 1; EXP188 item-incoterms on GPUs 4,6 with per-rank batch 2 wave 2.
First post-launch status: EXP186 is running, EXP187/188 are queued for later waves, and orchestrator recommends keep_watching.
Final metrics: EXP186 user-churn roc_auc=0.6841889309621224; EXP187 user-ltv mae=77.78193103098369; EXP188 item-incoterms mrr=0.6782075427827381.
Final verdict under replay-aware effective deltas: user-churn better, user-ltv neutral, item-incoterms worse; global verdict failed.
Throughput was acceptable: EXP188 ran on two ranks with per-rank batch 2 at about 6.5 train step/s, roughly 26 effective train items/sec, and final test ran around 12.07 it/s with val_size=1.

Interpretation: candidate-set group routing did not preserve EXP180's partial salt-side signal. It exactly repeated EXP183's item-incoterms collapse band, so further prompt evidence routing order variants should be treated as saturated unless a new mechanism changes the information path beyond routing/reordering existing tokens.

Search-space consequence: pause manual candidate-aware prompt evidence routing variants. EXP180 remains a fixed-screen failed but retune-plausible signal; EXP183 and EXP186 show that adding sample conditioning or preserving group order does not stabilize it. The next action should be architecture-consistency review rather than another scorer/routing small variant.
